rationale: The citing paper uses Thompson and Norton (2011) as direct theoretical support for a core premise: consumers may value many-feature products for ownership-based social utility, not just functional use. This idea is woven into the conceptual development of the having-versus-using account and reappears when discussing alternative satisfaction outcomes and public-use implications. Because the cited work helps anchor the paper's framing of why consumers prefer feature-rich options before use, the citation plays a meaningful role beyond a passing literature mention.
relation_description: The citing paper builds its conceptual setup on Thompson and Norton (2011) by treating social utility from owning feature-rich products as prior evidence for a having-focused evaluation mindset. It then develops its own argument that prompting usage estimation shifts decisions away from that having focus toward using considerations.
